segments.text: Hello everyone. Uh my name is Andrea
Malagodi. I'm the CTO at Sonosource.
I've been here for about five and a half
years
and I wanted to talk to you about three
things that I find um quite fascinating
at Sonar and I think hope that this is
also expressed in the products and the
innovations and features that we roll
out. When I arrived, I came from a
rather large corporate um institution,
global, 300,000 employees, 25,000 people
in technology. And I came into a company
that was based in Geneva where I work
with about 230 people at the time. And
there was a few things I witnessed that
were quite fascinating. And they're kind
of cultural markers that I would say
still go through today and still carry
through today and is true kind of in the
core of how we look at things and how we
consider things. Uh some of it is maybe
just a little uh you know quaint uh to
share but I thought I'd just uh talk you
through it.
The first thing that I find really
fascinating is that in the company every
Friday at 4:30 on the dots we open up a
zoom session for the whole company and
in that company we have in that uh zoom
session we have a a what we call demo
day it's about 30 minutes long open mic
anybody can basically rock up and
present what it is they have delivered
during that week and this is a very
energizing and super impressive
um cultural event that happens every
single week. And what's so very very
wonderful about it is it's um totally
unedited. Uh there's no planning around
it. It is literally we rock up 4:30 p.m.
Central European time. We start and all
global members basically attend uh and
present what they worked on. And we see
new innovations, we see experiments, we
see failures, we see customer cases
where people have helped uh all sorts.
And I feel this is really a great way of
being transparent and showing what it is
that's going on internally in the
company and make sure that everybody's
connected to the features that we
eventually roll out and hopefully serve
you well with. The second cultural um
trait I would say at Sonosource that I
was also very impressed with when I
arrived uh and it's still there today is
what we do in terms of dog fooding. Um,
so every single feature that we build
inside the company basically gets
announced in a bunch of Slack channels
that we have and the internal
developers,
product managers, whomever really wants
to basically can grab those features and
start exploring them. And we usually
have kind of a a Slack channel for each
of the features and people pummel in
with feedback, observations,
critique,
uh you know, good ideas based on other
good ideas, uh really to try to help
hone the feature and make sure it's
ready and you know, just see what it is
we haven't necessarily thought about and
get that feedback. And it's kind of the
best uh QA if you will because if you
have you know over 200 developers trying
out the features that we are rolling out
to you and to your customer instances uh
we are our own harshest critics in that
sense and even for features that we have
uh rolled out already. Uh we also have
eyes on that continuously. We raise
feedbacks internally in our channels for
that and product managers and
engineering teams that own those
features will grab that feedback and get
those corrections deployed out. If it's
our cloud product which we deploy
probably hundreds and hundreds of times
during a month, our server product that
comes out on steady releases, we'll have
those things addressed very rapidly. And
the third thing I just wanted to um
share with you is that uh many of our
customers have our premium support model
and raise obviously tickets and you know
issues that they have and our excellent
support team globally uh help you to
address those tickets as quickly as
possible. But at times there are things
that needs engineering help and
internally again we have slack channels
and juro tickets that gets raised and
they get a lot of eyes very very rapidly
and get attention and get help. So there
is always an ability for support to kind
of make a call to arms specifically if
there's something urgent that needs to
be dealt with uh something critical for
a customer and people flock around that
problem to get it resolved and get a
resolution rolled out to whatever the
case may be. And I think across the
entire organization there is a customer
f first focus um that helps us just be
very attentive to the needs and issues
that customers may run into to try to
come up with resolutions and help on any
incidents or issues they may run into.

fullText: Hello everyone. Uh my name is Andrea Malagodi. I'm the CTO at Sonosource. I've been here for about five and a half years and I wanted to talk to you about three things that I find um quite fascinating at Sonar and I think hope that this is also expressed in the products and the innovations and features that we roll out. When I arrived, I came from a rather large corporate um institution, global, 300,000 employees, 25,000 people in technology. And I came into a company that was based in Geneva where I work with about 230 people at the time. And there was a few things I witnessed that were quite fascinating. And they're kind of cultural markers that I would say still go through today and still carry through today and is true kind of in the core of how we look at things and how we consider things. Uh some of it is maybe just a little uh you know quaint uh to share but I thought I'd just uh talk you through it. The first thing that I find really fascinating is that in the company every Friday at 4:30 on the dots we open up a zoom session for the whole company and in that company we have in that uh zoom session we have a a what we call demo day it's about 30 minutes long open mic anybody can basically rock up and present what it is they have delivered during that week and this is a very energizing and super impressive um cultural event that happens every single week. And what's so very very wonderful about it is it's um totally unedited. Uh there's no planning around it. It is literally we rock up 4:30 p.m. Central European time. We start and all global members basically attend uh and present what they worked on. And we see new innovations, we see experiments, we see failures, we see customer cases where people have helped uh all sorts. And I feel this is really a great way of being transparent and showing what it is that's going on internally in the company and make sure that everybody's connected to the features that we eventually roll out and hopefully serve you well with. The second cultural um trait I would say at Sonosource that I was also very impressed with when I arrived uh and it's still there today is what we do in terms of dog fooding. Um, so every single feature that we build inside the company basically gets announced in a bunch of Slack channels that we have and the internal developers, product managers, whomever really wants to basically can grab those features and start exploring them. And we usually have kind of a a Slack channel for each of the features and people pummel in with feedback, observations, critique, uh you know, good ideas based on other good ideas, uh really to try to help hone the feature and make sure it's ready and you know, just see what it is we haven't necessarily thought about and get that feedback. And it's kind of the best uh QA if you will because if you have you know over 200 developers trying out the features that we are rolling out to you and to your customer instances uh we are our own harshest critics in that sense and even for features that we have uh rolled out already. Uh we also have eyes on that continuously. We raise feedbacks internally in our channels for that and product managers and engineering teams that own those features will grab that feedback and get those corrections deployed out. If it's our cloud product which we deploy probably hundreds and hundreds of times during a month, our server product that comes out on steady releases, we'll have those things addressed very rapidly. And the third thing I just wanted to um share with you is that uh many of our customers have our premium support model and raise obviously tickets and you know issues that they have and our excellent support team globally uh help you to address those tickets as quickly as possible. But at times there are things that needs engineering help and internally again we have slack channels and juro tickets that gets raised and they get a lot of eyes very very rapidly and get attention and get help. So there is always an ability for support to kind of make a call to arms specifically if there's something urgent that needs to be dealt with uh something critical for a customer and people flock around that problem to get it resolved and get a resolution rolled out to whatever the case may be. And I think across the entire organization there is a customer f first focus um that helps us just be very attentive to the needs and issues that customers may run into to try to come up with resolutions and help on any incidents or issues they may run into.
